reasoning: The 360-day average of ~129 'Battles' per 30 days comprises both dry-season peaks and wet-season lulls. In Sudan, the rainy season (June through late September) heavily constrains ground troop movement, causing armies to rely more on drones and artillery (which ACLED codes separately as 'Explosions/Remote violence' rather than 'Battles'). The first two resolution dates evaluate periods squarely within this rainy season, meaning battle counts are very likely to fall below the 12-month average. The latter two dates evaluate periods in the dry season (October–May) when ground offensives historically surge; an ongoing political escalation (e.g., the RSF's announcement of a parallel government in July 2025) suggests the conflict will remain intense. Therefore, dry season months are favored to exceed the 360-day average. Consistent with forecasting principles, probabilities are regressed toward 0.5 as the forecast horizon increases to account for long-term uncertainties like potential ceasefires or major paradigm shifts.
